If you and everyone else who reads this email makes just 10 calls to caucus-goers and voters right now -- we'll reach our goal with no problem. But we need you to get started right now: | | | [](https://my.elizabethwarren.com/page/m/2852a683/45c30650/1e3ee14d5/66fed702/133815099/VEsH/?g=4qwscq_HJ6FcsC0VbCsFfMA "https://elizabethwarren.com/call")

We're fast approaching our deadline to make one million calls -- and we're still a little behind where we need to be:

We still need to make 313,078 calls to hit our goal tonight.

**If you and everyone else who reads this email makes just 10 calls to caucus-goers and voters right now -- we'll reach this goal with no problem. But we need you to get started right now:** | | ****[**MAKE CALLS NOW**](https://my.elizabethwarren.com/page/m/2852a683/45c30650/1e3ee14d5/66fed705/133815099/VEsE/p/eyJlM3NnY21WbVkyOWtaU0I5ZlE9PSI6ImVtYWlsLTIwMjAwMTE5LW9uZW1pbGxpb25jYWxscy1mdWxsIn0%3D/?g=4qwscq_HJ6FcsC0VbCsFfMA "https://www.elizabethwarren.com/call/?utm_source=email-20200119-onemillioncalls-full")****

We're not pulling your leg when we share our organizing and fundraising goals with you. We want you to know how critical these deadlines are for the success of our grassroots movement -- and how, if we all pitch in, we can reach this big goal.

**With the Iowa caucuses in just 15 days, talking with voters and caucus-goers now -- sharing why we're in this fight and why they should be, too -- is how we'll out-organize, outlast, and WIN.**

**[Click here to start making calls now-- start with just 10 calls, then see how you feel. Every call you make gets us a little closer to reaching this important goal, and every voter or caucus-goer you talk with gets us closer to taking back the White House.](https://my.elizabethwarren.com/page/m/2852a683/45c30650/1e3ee14d5/66fed705/133815099/VEsF/p/eyJlM3NnY21WbVkyOWtaU0I5ZlE9PSI6ImVtYWlsLTIwMjAwMTE5LW9uZW1pbGxpb25jYWxscy1mdWxsIn0%3D/?g=4qwscq_HJ6FcsC0VbCsFfMA "https://www.elizabethwarren.com/call/?utm_source=email-20200119-onemillioncalls-full")**

Thanks for being in this fight with us. Now, let's hit the phones!

Team Warren

Hey team! I’m Zack, a senior advisor at Tom 2020 — right now I’m working with our team in Iowa to get ready for the caucuses. I met Tom on a cold morning in Des Moines back in January 2014, and as long as I’ve known him, he has always been willing to take on tough fights, tell the truth, and build coalitions that have empowered and lifted the voices of so many people. Tom and I agree that the right time to do the right thing is always right now, and for me, now is the time to stand with Tom. A lot happened at Tom 2020 this week — here are the highlights: 💡Tom lit up the stage at Tuesday’s Democratic debate. We were so glad he got to share his plans for the climate, the economy, and more! This was the last debate before the Iowa caucuses, and it was great for everyone to see what a strong candidate Tom is. body:
** LAST WEEK WITH ROB
------------------------------------------------------------
Dear friends,

Thank you for taking the time to join me for another “Last Week with Rob.” This week, I was able to spend time with an American war hero, the disastrous new state bail reforms claimed the life of an innocent young man, and we finally launched our first radio ad!

** An American Hero
------------------------------------------------------------

On Wednesday, I was able to spend some time with U.S. Army SSgt. David Bellavia as he visited Lockport to speak about his life since becoming a Medal of Honor recipient. He talked about our duty to help those who return from war adjust back into civilian life and the importance of fighting for what’s right. As a veteran of Operation Enduring Freedom, I appreciate David and what he is doing. There is no better ambassador for the U.S. Army or veterans than SSgt. Bellavia.

** NY Bail Reform Disaster Continues
------------------------------------------------------------

We’ve also had another week where we have seen the disastrous impact bail reform is having on New Yorkers. L (http://longisland.news12.com/story/41557445/bellport-man-released-without-bail-after-dwi-charge-in-deadly-william-floyd-parkway-crash?fbclid=IwAR2nRz2jMPfNyxz-EvNjzcq-2gMzpmH8NjWKhOmxne2S_0_kTWzLjgoHmbY) ast week (http://longisland.news12.com/story/41557445/bellport-man-released-without-bail-after-dwi-charge-in-deadly-william-floyd-parkway-crash?fbclid=IwAR2nRz2jMPfNyxz-EvNjzcq-2gMzpmH8NjWKhOmxne2S_0_kTWzLjgoHmbY) , news of a recent UB grad who lost his life due to the reckless actions of a repeat offender shook residents across the state. The driver, who had six prior felony convictions, six prior misdemeanor convictions, and five failures to appear in court on other cases was released on Jan. 1 2020, without bail thanks to the new bail reforms. Ten days later, he was drunk and crashed into Jonathan Armand Flores-Maldonadom, killing the Long Island native and recent UB grad.
Because of NY Democrats and their horrible bail reforms, this young man had his life taken. We must continue to fight back and repeal these dangerous new criminal reforms.

** Our First Radio Ad!
------------------------------------------------------------

Finally, last week, we launched our first campaign ad called “Fighter.” In case you haven’t heard it, click here (https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=pL-zUGGZKfw&=&feature=youtu.be&=&fbclid=IwAR1qalCy3nqDi6z7JTWAjKJOrLlppZL9p8-R68tZrbb-1GA_UUbY2wLGj2o) . President Trump has faced false attacks from the radical left since he first announced his campaign for President. Since taking office, the President has had to fight back against countless liberal regressive witch-hunts. I know what that’s like because those same crooked politicians attacked me for my conservative values. I stood up to politically motivated attacks from the disgraced Eric Schneiderman and I won - just as President Trump has done. Now, I want to go to Washington to fight by his side against this impeachment sham and help him advance the conservative agenda he was elected to enact.
As always, feel free to stay up to date on my campaign by following me on Facebook and Twitter at @RobOrttforCongress and let me know what’s going on in your community and your life. Thank you and God Bless.

Rob
Battle-tested Patriot

| | Friend, I have some disturbing news.

In November, the nation watched in amazement and fear as Democrats flipped the Virginia State legislature from red to blue and defeated a conservative governor in Kentucky. And it didn't take long for the Democrats to expose their anti-gun, anti-freedom agendas. In fact, the ink was barely dry on election results when the assault began on law-abiding gun owners of their state.

Over forty (40) anti-gun bills that have been proposed by the Virginia legislature. Its governor has even requested additional funds in his new budget to capture, prosecute and imprison gun owners who refuse to voluntarily give up their firearms. When asked how the gun confiscation plan would be enforced, Virginia Congressman Donald McEachin (D-VA) suggested the governor use the National Guard to go house-to-house confiscating firearms.

And it doesn't end in Virgina. Just this past week, two Democrat State Senators, Robert Jackson and David Jordan (MS), filed Red Flag legislation in the Senate.

The bottom line is, a point has been reached where government is willing to kill anyone who believes in our constitutional right to keep and bear arms. **I am doing what I can to prevent this from happening in Mississipppi by petitioning my county to become a second amendment sanctuary. But, with your help, we can petition more local governments to become a second amendment sanctuaries.**

It's up to us to stand firm on our belief in our constitution and draw a line in the sand that no government dare cross.

**If you are committed to the second amendment, I am asking you to join me by adding your name below** , as we petition local governments to pass a resolution desginating their county or municipality a Second Amendment Sanctuary, which:

* **Protects against any unconstitutional law that attempts to confiscate or disarm law-abiding citizens.**

* **Bars any tax dollars from being utilized to confiscate firearms of law-abiding citizens.**

* **Pledges to use all means available to defend citizens and law enforcement officers who refuse to abide by unconstitutional gun confiscation laws.**

name: Nina Ahmad
subject: A Day of Remembrance and Service
disclaimer_text: Paid for by Nina for PA
body:
[](https://act.myngp.com/el/Y3uNzo8j1ZYHHzxlYvtEVNxhwbEwCArnRz_QdlHSqqM=/TRmxhjKv9zc6z0axQfQ9FinTKk5hII_h957m8TvJ7oU=) | | Today, we take a moment to celebrate the life of Dr. Martin Luther King, Jr. Dr. King was a civil rights icon who fought to end racial inequality and bring economic justice for all. But we have yet to reach the "Promised Land" and his vision remains relevant to disenfranchised, persecuted and marginalized people across the world. His dedication to the ideals of justice and equality have resonated throughout my life. As a first-generation immigrant who came to this country for a better life I have strived to live up to his ideals. Now, more than ever, let us embrace his memory and strive to build the world that he envisioned. Happy Martin Luther King Day! Let us never forget Dr. King's message and vision for America. **_ "Injustice anywhere is a threat to justice everywhere. We are caught in an inescapable network of mutuality, tied in a single garment of destiny. name: Sri Preston Kulkarni
subject: Hate does not drive out hate
disclaimer_text: Paid for by Sri for Congress
body:
[](https://act.myngp.com/el/C1dPT3wvaQhZJaW2biPNj-ZEbY7cQviPs-3010ITE0I=/lTRGLK9IpkljHr-TNrOucory-Y7hDmzs82A3p1haPPg=)
---
Team,

Today is both a somber and hopeful day. On MLK Day, we remember the movement that Martin Luther King, Jr. led at a time when even basic civil rights for all races in America were not recognized and that he faced violence, imprisonment, and eventually death fighting for this cause.

We also recognize that his method of organizing, his commitment to nonviolent resistance, and his ability to unify people across divides is still relevant today when elected leaders are exploiting racial and religious tension for political advantage.

When we see politicians saying that people should be banned from the United States or prevented from holding office based on their religion, when the people with the authority to pick our judges say that certain ethnicities cannot be their judge (and then nominate almost no judges of those ethnicities), when female Members of Congress of color are told to go back to their country, when our first African-American President is accused of faking his birth certificate, when people are getting their voting rights stripped away based upon where they live and their or their country of origin, when white nationalist terror attacks have killed minorities in churches, synagogues, temples, and Wal-Mart, or when members of one political party are openly admitting to being white nationalists, we may be tempted to wonder what happened to Dr. King's dream. Our first instinct may be to simply get angry at a certain party or certain elected officials. Or worse, we may feel like throwing up our hands and giving up.

But, we must never forget Dr. King's words, that "Hate does not drive out hate, only love can do that." The way to defeat this wave of anger and division is not with more anger and division, but with more compassion and inclusion. When people feel that democracy has become ugly, that our government does not represent them, and they have no power to make positive change, they stop participating, and this gives more power to the worst actors in our society. Our goal must be to be more unifying, more energizing, more inspiring than those who want us to tune out. That is the only way to preserve Dr. King's dream.

Destroying bridges is easy. Building them is much harder, and takes more time. But build we must. The arc of the moral universe is long, but it bends toward justice.

Sri Preston Kulkarni

name: Ben Salango
subject: Dr. Martin Luther King
body:
Martin Luther King was a hero.
_**“Life's most persistent and urgent question is, 'What are you doing for others?'”
\- Dr. Martin Luther King Jr.**_
---

Team,
60 years ago this very week, Dr. Martin Luther King visited First Baptist Church in Charleston, WV and delivered his message of hope and equality.
Today, we celebrate courage, resilience, and service - the values Dr. King and the heroes of the civil rights movement modeled for us. The life and legacy of Dr. King continues to inspire leaders to stand up for what’s right.
He never lost hope when the odds seemed impossible. Let’s honor Dr. King today and every day by never backing down from a fight we know is critical to all working families.
Sincerely,
Ben Salango
Ben Salango for WV

name: Charles Booker
subject: The legacy of Dr. Martin Luther King Jr.

body:

---
Folks, On this celebration of Martin Luther King Jr. Day, we must stop and take a moment to reflect on the immeasurable changes Dr. King catalyzed in the struggle for racial equality and equity during his time on this planet, which was cut far too short. Dr. King’s leadership changed our country and the world. He blazed the trail I stand on today. As the youngest black state legislator in Kentucky in nearly 90 years, I stand on his shoulders and those of other civil rights leaders. In the last years of his life, Dr. King’s focus expanded as he began the next chapter of his work in the struggle for equality: The Poor People’s Campaign, a movement that sought to build a multiracial coalition of economically disadvantaged people. The premise was simple: The government can — and should — make sure every American has the dignity and security of a guaranteed basic income, and that no one is exploited and left behind by the economy ... or left stranded on the tracks. Today, the idea of a Universal Basic Income is being discussed more and more, including in the Democratic presidential primary. And it’s a conversation we need to be having in Kentucky, too. When I think about our Commonwealth and the people I serve, and the millions I hope to serve soon in the United States Senate, it is hard to not see how the Universal Basic Income — championed by Dr. King — would help the people of Kentucky. The only way we can give people true pathways to make decisions in their lives is by guaranteeing them more disposable income so they have the freedom to invent, to start a business, or advance their education. This is not a handout; it is an overdue and necessary investment in the people of our Commonwealth and this country. I want to make sure every Kentuckian is compensated for the work they do, the productivity they create, and the sacrifices they make. I believe the best way to make that happen is by honoring Dr. King’s legacy and finishing what he started. Today, we must commit ourselves to fighting for a Universal Basic Income. Glad to have you in this fight. name: Peter Khalil
subject: What will be our legacy?
disclaimer_text: Paid for by the Committee to Elect Peter Khalil
body:
| | | | The Civil Rights Movement Isn't Over.
---
---
**—**
Everything has been said and written about Martin Luther King. His legacy is larger than life. So large does Dr. King loom in our American consciousness that we sometimes forget about his humanity. His love for people. His commitment to achieving the equality of opportunity that remains the undelivered promise of our nation.
The Civil Rights Movement is not over. In many ways, we are still segregated, if not by law, then by circumstance. The road ahead calls for decisive action. Only through action can we finally fulfill the American promise.
I often reflect on what our most revered historical figures would think if they were alive today. What would Dr. King think about our modern society? What would he think about our divisions? About our treatment of newcomers to this country? About our treatment of our minority and marginalized citizens? Our disabled citizens? Our native people? Our treatment of our fellow Americans who may not conform to historical gender norms?
This MLK day, I urge you to reflect on who we are as a society. On how far we've come and on how much we have left to accomplish. Think about how you want our country to look in five years. Ten years. A quarter-century. Think about what our descendants will say about us and whether we are doing all we can to create a legacy that we can be proud of.
This MLK day, I hope you will reflect on this moment. This moment in our history where we decide whether we move forward or whether we give in to the forces that would have us remain a house divided.
With hope for a better future,
Peter

clean_body: | | | | The Civil Rights Movement Isn't Over. — Everything has been said and written about Martin Luther King. His legacy is larger than life. So large does Dr. King loom in our American consciousness that we sometimes forget about his humanity. His love for people. His commitment to achieving the equality of opportunity that remains the undelivered promise of our nation. The Civil Rights Movement is not over. In many ways, we are still segregated, if not by law, then by circumstance. The road ahead calls for decisive action. Only through action can we finally fulfill the American promise. I often reflect on what our most revered historical figures would think if they were alive today. What would Dr. King think about our modern society? What would he think about our divisions? About our treatment of newcomers to this country? About our treatment of our minority and marginalized citizens? Our disabled citizens? Our native people? Our treatment of our fellow Americans who may not conform to historical gender norms? This MLK day, I urge you to reflect on who we are as a society. On how far we've come and on how much we have left to accomplish. Think about how you want our country to look in five years. Ten years. A quarter-century. Think about what our descendants will say about us and whether we are doing all we can to create a legacy that we can be proud of. This MLK day, I hope you will reflect on this moment. This moment in our history where we decide whether we move forward or whether we give in to the forces that would have us remain a house divided. With hope for a better future, Peter Fuel the Progressive movement in SW Washington and help us get an office by becoming a sustaining donor! | Paid for by the Committee to Elect Peter Khalil

Folks, **Today we honor Dr. Martin Luther King Jr. and the movement he led for dignity, equality, and justice.** We honor Dr. King by recommitting ourselves to his vision of an America where all children can join hands and play, by recognizing the heroes of the Civil Rights Movement who continue to lead us today, and by taking part in acts of service -- for our people, for our country, and for our planet. Dr. King and his colleagues fought to secure the right to vote and end segregation in all its forms. We fight iterations of these same battles today as Republicans continue to attack voting rights, condone unjust housing and lending practices that keep communities segregated, and support policies that incarcerate black men and debilitate black communities. In honor of the life, legacy, and vision of Dr. Martin Luther King Jr., I promise to continue fighting until his dream is our reality. I am grateful, as always, to have you with me in this fight. name: Andrew Young
subject: On Dr. King’s legacy
disclaimer_text: PAID FOR BY TERESA TOMLINSON FOR SENATE
body:
Friend,

As we honor the life and legacy of my late friend Dr. Martin Luther King, I wanted to offer you my thoughts on where our struggle for equal rights stands today.

When I first became involved in the Civil Rights Movement some sixty years ago, we sought to transform the civic and economic systems of this country. There is an urgent need to renew that effort today. We recognize that systems of oppression continue to fall along racial and economic divides, and changing those divides require building solidarity across many disparate groups.

We have the potential to build such a coalition in this crucial election year.

Dr. King worked to build a multiracial, multiethnic, socioeconomically diverse movement, bridging the rural and urban divide - a movement that welcomes all people on the basis of love and respect. We must continue this important work.

In 1968, when Dr. King was assassinated in Memphis, Tennessee, I was there. We were supporting sanitation workers striking for fair wages and improved working conditions. Martin understood the need for solidarity in the face of racial, civic and economic injustice.

Today, we live in a country where people working a full-time job may still live in poverty due to the absence of livable wage legislation, the assault on unions and collective bargaining, and economic policies that shrink the middle-class.

We also live in a country where over 300,000 Georgians can be purged from the voter rolls simply for not voting — as if this inalienable right has an expiration date - and where citizens having paid their debt to society face a poll tax in Florida just for access to the ballot box.

We can and must do better!

Our struggle for equity in housing, education, employment, prosperity and civic life continues. We must find strong, thoughtful, respectful leaders willing to do the hard work of bringing us closer to justice. I believe that Teresa Tomlinson is such a leader. Her U.S. Senate campaign celebrates justice for all, and specifically includes addressing a new Civic and Economic Infrastructure. [https://teresatomlinson.com/home/policy-paper-a-new-civic-and-economic-infrastructure/]

Dr. King’s legacy was handed to each of us. Our work is not done. Indeed, the work to create a more perfect union never ceases, and it will take all of us to win the morally just society we deserve. Stay focused. Remain committed. Together, the power we hold can move mountains.

Gratefully,
Andrew Young

| Dear Patriot,
Today as a nation we take time to reflect and celebrate the life of Martin Luther King, Jr. Dr. King conducted his work in peaceful and respectful ways that encouraged a nation to become united. His vision transcends race to the true nature of our character as God's children and gives us a message of love and hope.
Let's not forget this message, whether you are participating in a day of service, a prayer meeting, or just committing a random act of kindness. Remember those who have gone before us so that we may all enjoy the fruits of our nation, "One Country Under God, Indivisible with Liberty and Justice for All."

disclaimer_text: Paid for by DePasquale for PA 10
body:
Today, we celebrate the life and legacy of Dr. Martin Luther King, Jr. Throughout his life, Dr. King fought for a more fair and equitable society. His legacy continues to inspire us today and will continue to inspire generations to come.
[](https://click.ngpvan.com/k/14103827/147457618/1960319551?nvep=ew0KICAiVGVuYW50VXJpIjogIm5ncHZhbjovL3Zhbi9OR1AvTkdQMDcvMS83NTE3NyIsDQogICJEaXN0cmlidXRpb25VbmlxdWVJZCI6ICJmYzE5YmQzNC1hYzNiLWVhMTEtYTFjYy0yODE4Nzg0ZDA4NGYiLA0KICAiRW1haWxBZGRyZXNzIjogImRwd2lsbGlzNjdAZ21haWwuY29tIg0KfQ%3D%3D&hmac=mBsRT1lB2qtp3mtA-eAKKlojWfm-uU7zWksQ_Wi972s=&emci=db477b00-a33b-ea11-a1cc-2818784d084f&emdi=fc19bd34-ac3b-ea11-a1cc-2818784d084f&ceid=10203249) Friend,

Today, we celebrate the life and legacy of Dr. Martin Luther King, Jr. Throughout his life, Dr. King fought for a more fair and equitable society. His legacy continues to inspire us today and will continue to inspire generations to come.

There is no doubt that we are all better off because of Dr. King. We must continue his work in fighting for the values he held so dearly -- equal opportunity and justice for all. As Dr. King said, **“Life’s most persistent and urgent question is, ‘What are you doing for others?’”**

We can all draw inspiration from his words as we honor his legacy, today and every day.

Thank you,
Eugene

We must continue his work in fighting for the values he held so dearly -- equal opportunity and justice for all. As Dr. King said, “Life’s most persistent and urgent question is, ‘What are you doing for others?’” We can all draw inspiration from his words as we honor his legacy, today and every day. Today we honor the memory of Dr. Martin Luther King, Jr., who helped transform our nation and carry us forward in the yet unfinished fight for social justice and equity for all Americans. | | |
[](https://u1584542.ct.sendgrid.net/mps2/c/HwE/ni0YAA/t.2yk/26c3ZG71S8qY9FsDCJHflQ/h0/hhRpZZW1RfrqdNP9De00vQRlUIk-2BKhU3wA9r6CKglNJvIY0UI8PBiux5mOP3HzqwRIc8ctk0G-2B84LyI4ptr1FZgOUwMHXR7BfqLPmsOwPJpfMrFCZvnYopbISW-2FKFBxPXrzsUr0DQbbvJuc0XuZsPUNSA2ssqShQQ1Mn07EF5PwAu7g66-2Bdd1j27a7wvecp8RR6hFMULKKPCy9hMKG5NhDyxirGY-2FxNFQVKgO9BagZ1fUP-2F5Lu-2FcRTqD-2Fk1DKMBodG7jquu5PVuAMDOsFqWBQHcfaKg6wqbkQbqtBlG3Y8E-3D/eSd9)
Friend, Today we honor the memory of Dr. Martin Luther King, Jr., who helped transform our nation and carry us forward in the yet unfinished fight for social justice and equity for all Americans. As I reflect on King’s legacy, I hope we will remember not only the Dr. King who is taught in history books, but also the Dr. King who, before his assassination, radically called for the liberation of poor people throughout our country and stood with workers and the oppressed in their fight for economic justice.
I think of the Poor People’s Campaign, in which Dr. King brought together tens of thousands to take to the streets and demand an end to poverty in America. Due to his untimely death weeks earlier, King was unable to attend that march.
I think of the Dr. King who said, “Of all the forms of inequality, injustice in health is the most shocking and the most inhuman because it often results in physical death.” I remember the Dr. King who was regarded in his time not only as a great unifier, but in many communities throughout the country as a subversive radical who was tearing the country apart simply by demanding that the richest nation on earth satisfy the basic material needs of the poorest among us.
When we are told that we are “too extreme” for demanding health care for every American, a living wage so that people can provide for themselves and their families, and a Green New Deal to save our planet from climate catastrophe, I think of Dr. King. He did not let the pessimists and naysayers deter him in his fight for true economic and social justice, and neither will we.
Yours in the Struggle,
Mondaire

I think of the Poor People’s Campaign, in which Dr. King brought together tens of thousands to take to the streets and demand an end to poverty in America. Due to his untimely death weeks earlier, King was unable to attend that march. I think of the Dr. King who said, “Of all the forms of inequality, injustice in health is the most shocking and the most inhuman because it often results in physical death.” I remember the Dr. King who was regarded in his time not only as a great unifier, but in many communities throughout the country as a subversive radical who was tearing the country apart simply by demanding that the richest nation on earth satisfy the basic material needs of the poorest among us. When we are told that we are “too extreme” for demanding health care for every American, a living wage so that people can provide for themselves and their families, and a Green New Deal to save our planet from climate catastrophe, I think of Dr. King. He did not let the pessimists and naysayers deter him in his fight for true economic and social justice, and neither will we. Yours in the Struggle, Mondaire ** ** | | Paid for by Mondaire for Congress Our mailing address is: Mondaire for Congress P.O. Box 933, Nyack, NY 10960 | | Sent via . name: Jason Yates
subject: MLK’s challenge to Christians
body:
| | There is a higher reality to which we must be conformed
---
[ ](https://www2.myfaithvotes.org/r?u=cPut9WozS2VYDdXxJVkfhob6Z1e1IDW4Ql8IwU80F5PI5Pu2yGxkok4z8QdVWguyNxAcmemPM7UY1zK8SPFq04SR1__lR17w5hLn81NTsNDKnPFpxcAcveIXKeN4vqrjInE0d1M-uggKFYa5zRJi2Q&e=be53806c85fc17bd685e1e4c4d896e92&utm_source=myfaithvotes&utm_medium=email&utm_campaign=e200120com01&n=1)
| Peter, Today, we honor and remember the legacy of Rev. Martin Luther King Jr. With a burning, righteous conviction Rev. King called America to account for centuries-old systems of abuse, racism, and inequality. Faith undergirded and amplified his prophetic voice as he bid Americans of every faith, color, stripe, and creed to walk shoulder-to-shoulder into a promised land of unity. We believe the best way to honor his legacy is to emulate his example. In one of Rev. King’s [early sermons](https://www2.myfaithvotes.org/r?u=cPut9WozS2VYDdXxJVkfhob6Z1e1IDW4Ql8IwU80F5PI5Pu2yGxkok4z8QdVWguyNxAcmemPM7UY1zK8SPFq04SR1__lR17w5hLn81NTsNDKnPFpxcAcveIXKeN4vqrjInE0d1M-uggKFYa5zRJi2Q&e=be53806c85fc17bd685e1e4c4d896e92&utm_source=myfaithvotes&utm_medium=email&utm_campaign=e200120com01&n=2) he said, “We as Christians have a mandate to be nonconformists. The Apostle Paul, a man who knew the reality of the Christian faith, counseled us, ‘Be not conformed to this world, but be ye transformed by the renewing of your mind.’ We as Christians are commanded to live differently. We are called to be people of conviction and not conformity; people of moral nobility and not social respectability. We are called to a higher loyalty, to a more excellent way.” Rev. King challenged Christians to be ambassadors for Kingdom principles, and it’s a charge that is still applicable to believers today. “We are sent out as pioneers to imbue an unchristian world with the ideals and way of living of a higher order and a more noble realm. Even though we live in the colony of time we are ultimately responsible to the empire of eternity. In other words, as Christians we must never give our ultimate loyalty to any time-bound custom or idea of earth. There is a higher reality at the heart of our universe to which we must be conformed—God and his kingdom of love.” Just as Rev. Martin Luther King Jr. was called to bring biblical principles forward in his generation, we are called to be advocates of God’s principles in ours. **United. We Stand.** Jason Yates
CEO

name: Elizabeth Warren
subject: The legacy of Dr. Martin Luther King, Jr.
disclaimer_text: Paid for by Warren for President
body:
Today, as a nation, we honor the legacy of Dr. Martin Luther King, Jr. We honor his struggles and his triumphs, his courage and his conviction. We honor the life he devoted to taking a stand... | | | [](https://my.elizabethwarren.com/page/m/2852a6b8/45c30650/1e3b0154c/66fed0b7/4181715344/VEsH/p/eyJKU1ZsYldGcGJDVWwiOiJkcHdpbGxpczY3QGdtYWlsLmNvbSIsIkpTVm1hWEp6ZEc1aGJXVWxKUT09IjoiIiwiSlNWc1lYTjBibUZ0WlNVbCI6IiIsIkpTVjZhWEFsSlE9PSI6IjE4MzI2In0%3D/?g=4qwscq_HJ6FcsC0VbCsFfMA "https://elizabethwarren.com?source=em-header&email=dpwillis67@gmail.com&firstname=&lastname=&zip=18326")
---
Today, as a nation, we honor the legacy of Dr. Martin Luther King, Jr.

We honor his struggles and his triumphs, his courage and his conviction. We honor the life he devoted to taking a stand against racial and economic injustice, and how by doing so, he forever changed the course of history.

Dr. King was the heart and hope of the civil rights movement. His dream shocked the conscience of our nation and challenged us to live up to our highest ideals.

As I've traveled the country on this campaign, I have seen first-hand the dream that Dr. King imagined so many years ago. I've talked with Americans of all ages and backgrounds, from red and blue and purple states. I've met with parents in big cities and young people in small towns. And while I've heard plenty about what's gone wrong in this country, what I hear about most is hope that we can build something better.

**That hope for a better America is Dr. King's legacy, and today we are soldiers in the fight for social, racial and economic justice.**

And make no mistake about it -- we are in a fight.

We're in a fight against Donald Trump and his Republican Party's politics of division.

A fight to clean up corruption in Washington and create an economy and a government that works for all of us.

A fight to tear down the legacy of systemic racism and discrimination and give everyone a voice in our democracy.

**These fights are all of our fights. And I'm running for president because I believe the only way we can reach a better America is by fighting together.**

As we honor Dr. King today, may we all remember that we are the heirs of his legacy. This moment in history comes to us, and we cannot pass it along to someone else. We must act. We, too, must fight back.

That's what Dr. King called on us to do. That's what the urgency of this moment requires us to do. And that is the way we will make Dr. King's dream a reality for us all.

**I know this is an uphill battle, but I believe we're called to this fight. And I believe that if we work together, fight together, and, yes, persist together -- we can overcome.**

Thanks for being a part of this,

Elizabeth

name: West Virginia Democratic Party
subject: We must remember the words of Dr. Martin Luther King Jr.
disclaimer_text: Paid for and authorized by the WV State Democratic Executive Committee (www
body:
[https://nvlupin.blob.core.windows.net/images/van/NGP/NGP02/1/67825/images/Screenshot%202020-01-20%2014.32.13.png]

Today, we honor Dr. Martin Luther King, but most importantly we promise to continue to fight forward. Dr. King's fight for justice, equality, and civil rights is just as important today as it was then. It's imperative that we do not allow those in charge to take us back; to discriminate; to fuel hate that divides us.

We're in a time where it feels like everything is going backward, and that includes everything from equality to obstacles at the ballot box, and even education.

Dr. King was a strong advocate for education and this morning we heard from our Democratic lawmakers about priorities moving forward to protect it. The City of Charleston gathered this afternoon for an honorary street dedication to Dr. King.

As we come together to honor his legacy and re-commit to uphold all that he fought for, we must remember his words.

Dr. King once said, "Life's most persistent and urgent question is: 'What are you doing for others?'"

We must ask ourselves what are we doing as lawmakers, as representatives, as neighbors, and fellow Mountaineers to help and protect one another.

name: Senator Erica Smith
subject: MLK Jr Day - Remembrance - Celebration - Service
disclaimer_text: Paid for by Erica for US
body:
Dear Supporter,

Today, we remember and honor the life of the Dr. Martin Luther King Jr. Dr. King embodied the heart and soul of our nation and the world with his unswerving commitment to non-violent social change, equity for people of all colors, and a passionate advocacy for the downtrodden.

Today, we celebrate Dr. King's life-long activism in the Civil Rights Movement, as a leader in the effort to end racial segregation, and as a true champion for racial equity for all.

Today, we commit to serve by volunteering our time, talent, and resources to help make a difference in the lives of those in our communities, our state, our nation, and the world.

On this day of remembrance, celebration, and service, Dr. King's words ring as true today as when he first shared them in saying, "The ultimate measure of a man is not where he stands in moments of comfort and convenience, but where he stands at times of challenge and controversy." He additionally shared, "Injustice anywhere is a threat to justice everywhere." Let us all strive to live our lives by the example of the life of Dr. Martin Luther King Jr.

Have a safe and blessed Martin Luther King Jr. Day.

{{Authorized and Paid for by Erica for US .}}

{{Erica For US P.O. Box 1236 Gaston, North Carolina 27832}}

Box 1236 Gaston, North Carolina 27832}} If you believe you received this message in error or wish to no longer receive email from us, please =.

name: Michael Bennet
subject: The march continues
disclaimer_text: Paid for by Bennet for America
body:
| | | **" We have also come to this hallowed spot to remind America of the fierce urgency of now. This is no time to engage in the luxury of cooling off or to take the tranquilizing drug of gradualism. Now is the time to make real the promises of democracy."**
-- Martin Luther King Jr. "I Have A Dream" speech in Washington, D.C., 1963

Friend,

During one of the darkest moments in our country's history, Dr. Martin Luther King Jr. led a movement that was carried from bloodied streets to the heart of our nation, inspiring so many to take up the torch of progress.

As Congressman John Lewis -- a storied hero in his own right -- once said, "Of all the gifts given to us by Dr. Martin Luther King Jr., I think the greatest has been the belief in society's ability to change and the power each of us has to affect that change."

I could not agree more, friend.

Dr. King is just as much a Founder as those who wrote our Constitution. Because all who demand a more perfect union and do the hard work of making it so are Founders.

Today, as the Trump administration does all it can to turn back the clock, we must rise to meet this historic moment with courage and conviction.

Dr. King, of anyone, had reason to feel cynical. Instead, he chose hope.

In these challenging times, we too could be cynical, but instead we must heed his calls to hold deep our faith in the future. We must remain steadfast in our shared conviction that the universe is on the side of justice. Every day, in this fight for a more perfect union, we must make real the promises of our democracy for all - and so the march continues.

Friend, I'm going to keep fighting. I hope you will join me.

Michael

subject: Sara, Amy, Theresa, and me
disclaimer_text: Paid for by MJ for Texas
body:

[](https://secure.mjfortexas.com/page/m/64890736/7f51f7bc/4aa9f761/4ef0099d/3556646729/VEsH/?g=f7VweDpTVzRduSR3BPpGv5A "https://secure.actblue.com/donate/logo?refcode=emaillogo&amount=5")

| | Hi Peter --

I've spent a lot of my life doing things people told me I couldn't or shouldn't -- joining the Air Force, becoming a pilot, taking on the Pentagon to open ground combat roles for women, and yes, running for Senate. Oftentimes, they didn't think I could or should because I'm a woman. But I did what I believed in anyway -- and I'm proud of what I've accomplished.

**Today, I 'm proud to stand with three strong women running bold campaigns for Senate: Sara Gideon in Maine, Amy McGrath in Kentucky, and Theresa Greenfield in Iowa.**

**[Will you pitch in to support these women running for Senate? Your donation will be split between Sara, Amy, Theresa, and me to power our campaigns.](https://secure.mjfortexas.com/page/m/64890736/7f51f7bc/4aa9f761/4ef0099c/3556646729/VEsE/p/eyJKU1ZOUVVsTVNVNUhTVVFsSlE9PSI6Ijc3NCJ9/?g=f7VweDpTVzRduSR3BPpGv5A "https://secure.actblue.com/donate/mj-hegargideonmcgrathgreenfield?refcode=email_20200120.1nd__774__&amount=10&recurring=true")** [**DONATE**](https://secure.mjfortexas.com/page/m/64890736/7f51f7bc/4aa9f761/4ef0099c/3556646729/VEsF/p/eyJKU1ZOUVVsTVNVNUhTVVFsSlE9PSI6Ijc3NCJ9/?g=f7VweDpTVzRduSR3BPpGv5A "https://secure.actblue.com/donate/mj-hegargideonmcgrathgreenfield?refcode=email_20200120.1nd__774__&amount=10&recurring=true") Each of these women is running to build a better future for our children. I know they care as much as I do about lowering prescription drug prices, preserving protections for people with preexisting conditions, and ending the gun violence epidemic.

But we won't accomplish any of these things without electing these strong, smart, and principled women in Maine, Kentucky, and Iowa -- and defeating Sen. Cornyn here in Texas.

Mitch McConnell won't even allow the Senate to _vote_ on some of the most important issues facing our country today, and Sen. Cornyn is his right-hand man in the extreme partisanship and gridlock. That's why **right now** is the time to build a new Senate that actually _works_ for the people, NOT the special interests or D.C. bosses.

**So will you chip in right now to support Sara, Amy, Theresa, and me as we fight to build a better Senate,_and_ a better future for our children?** | _If you've saved your payment information with ActBlue Express, your donation will go through immediately and be split between the four candidates._

name: Roe v. Wade Alert
subject: Breaking from The New York Times
disclaimer_text: Paid for by Democratic Governors Association
body:
| | |
---
| | **The New York Times: "More Than 200 Republicans Urge Supreme Court to Weigh Overturning Roe v. Wade"**
---
**Tell us before 11:59 p.m. - Do you support Roe v. Wade?**
---
| | | [YES](http://links.dga.mkt6018.com/ctt?kn=8&ms=MTY4NTEyNjYS1&r=NDgzNzk3NjA4NzczS0&b=0&j=MTgwMTI1MTA0MwS2&mt=1&rt=0)
---
| [NO](http://links.dga.mkt6018.com/ctt?kn=5&ms=MTY4NTEyNjYS1&r=NDgzNzk3NjA4NzczS0&b=0&j=MTgwMTI1MTA0MwS2&mt=1&rt=0)
---
**Women 's rights are under attack again, Team!** More than 200 GOP members of Congress called on the Supreme Court to overturn Roe v. Wade and deny women the fundamental right to choose a safe abortion.

name: Jay Inslee
subject: Why we need to talk about climate justice on MLK Day
disclaimer_text: PAID FOR BY JAY INSLEE FOR WASHINGTON (D)
body:
Frontline communities need action now.
| [](https://act.jayinslee.com/sign/mlk-climate-justice/?utm_source=ema_2020-01-20_mlk&source=ema_2020-01-20_mlk&utm_campaign=mlk&utm_medium=ema&t=3&akid=8024%2E868730%2EoIYHme) Friend, Dr. King once said, "Injustice anywhere is a threat to justice everywhere." That's why we can't ignore what's happening in our frontline communities: Climate change is our biggest existential threat as a nation, and it's widening the gulf of inequality between us. Communities of color and low-income communities are hit first and worst by climate change, and that's why justice must be at the center of any plan to combat the climate crisis. **We cannot let this injustice go unnoticed -- which is why I am not stopping!** [**I'm asking all 2020 Democratic presidential candidates to take up my Community Climate Justice plan to advance environmental, economic, and racial justice to build an inclusive clean energy economy for all -- add your name to join me.**](https://act.jayinslee.com/sign/mlk-climate-justice/?utm_source=ema_2020-01-20_mlk&source=ema_2020-01-20_mlk&utm_campaign=mlk&utm_medium=ema&t=4&akid=8024%2E868730%2EoIYHme) | [ADD YOUR NAME!](https://act.jayinslee.com/sign/mlk-climate-justice/?utm_source=ema_2020-01-20_mlk&source=ema_2020-01-20_mlk&utm_campaign=mlk&utm_medium=ema&t=5&akid=8024%2E868730%2EoIYHme)
---
Recent studies have shown that communities of color, including Hispanic and black communities, suffer from more harmful air pollution than their white counterparts.
Not only that: Historically "redlined" communities -- communities of color that have faced housing discrimination -- suffer double the amount of air pollution and 2.4 times the number of hospital admissions for asthma compared to other neighborhoods in the same cities.
The facts are clear: The impacts of climate change and pollution disproportionately hurt marginalized communities, and they are major contributing factors in ongoing racial and income inequality.
I hope candidates celebrating the life and legacy of Dr. King today heed his words and show communities of color that they're taking the lead on environmental justice and will prioritize community-led solutions. We can't wait, and our friends and neighbors need to know this is a priority.
[**Defeating the climate crisis is about leveling the playing field, and that's why I'm calling on all 2020 Democratic presidential candidates to prioritize my community-led climate justice plan: Add your name to join in.**](https://act.jayinslee.com/sign/mlk-climate-justice/?utm_source=ema_2020-01-20_mlk&source=ema_2020-01-20_mlk&utm_campaign=mlk&utm_medium=ema&t=6&akid=8024%2E868730%2EoIYHme)
Very truly yours,
Jay

Dr. Martin Luther King Jr. and the civil rights movement, it's important to remember that the work these pioneers started so many decades ago is not done. Ending segregation and discrimination in housing, schools, public transit, and so many other spaces is something we are still struggling to achieve. The fight for voting rights has been particularly difficult. Across the country, GOP legislatures are using a Supreme Court ruling that weakened the Voting Rights Act of 1965 to dismantle suffrage and recreate old barriers to voting. In Florida, voting rights activists like the Florida Rights Restoration Coalition were able to achieve a rare victory in the South, championing voter-approved Amendment 4, which restored voting rights to former felons. Almost immediately, however, the GOP-led legislature and governor passed a law requiring that former felons pay off all of their court fees and fines before they can vote \-- a modern poll tax. Florida's state Supreme Court just upheld this law last week. So now, the Florida Rights Restoration Coalition is working to get as many former felons enfranchised as possible by helping them pay their fines and fees. But they need you to continue their work. Today, you can help expand voting rights. You can ensure more voices are heard in government. You can push back against GOP voter suppression. This isn’t the America Dr. Martin Luther King, Jr. fought for, friend. For too many Americans, racial justice, economic equality, livable wages, fair working conditions, and opportunities for growth and prosperity are still out of reach. More than 50 years after Dr. King's death, we’re still fighting for these same things. They are key drivers on this campaign, guiding our policies and vision for our nation. This isn’t a fight we can just give up on. We honor the contributions of Dr. King — a leader who forever changed our history by calling for African-American civil rights, equality _and_ equity — by embodying his values of service and sacrifice today, and every day. African Americans built this nation and shaped our values without receiving any gratitude or recompense. So it’s on all of us — but especially those who continue to benefit from whiteness and privilege in society — to get out and give back. **Volunteer in your community and do what you can to make your corner of the world brighter, more fair, and equal for all.** [_**Click here to find a place to volunteer near you.**_](http://clicks.tomsteyer.com/wf/click?upn=cBSXlkRq21E9V5onLqLCBg4-2Bu0eLGSZQL5q4RmC-2B5V9edjMXyK8wnbohZMwhKDvvPOHClLgTeD-2BeBa35Wb7PeXn3d-2FGeOGcKUy30im-2F6P98-3D_enNSH-2FxHAAqXgxiHRFihpX91d75ERAkpfg4XQfmYhCEfMDVgenuIpuaqOZjDID1RvhNGhYRMastDcDetuZ96NF8hToF5DJMJQJ5Sm-2F8GsIIPCrHOtDSQWCFJE3mkY7M8jsZJO1oJBZhFIJLFixaMiWrvm8RmUVxAM-2BNP2wfmkYbXsDsXT21fzaAP2ZREvvlavoscaqoBrjoMIv9wbkmULDmchguWMM99dg1YPpsFBk8-2Bqmm47NItxCN2uzT86AC9bX9aOeIzLf4DXzxGAR3C-2F-2BSdVnuy16xoCg62L8v9MvQ2drbCsJT-2FPNj7uPc-2BXCqXlItpFU9s4uyb4eOH88gztfoObX-2BR7HRBnhTAIaCkJTah9qQlA-2FlTV3blyJWO-2BXAb4IZ8-2FmfAOmUVcRQhTrDex1eyFC6HbKC7WhinWOy2PgCJVzS13ZQS0wBVrtgo21VyZu6ZlV3RA6yK4JnOZoqIjb3cJNnR8BEnTo-2FMfsDaXoWAxV-2Fz2jooxv-2Fm1XaFFb8a-2FgREtDHwZZf8YFm7qxF8D5kfUexx3qaa678-2BmhjOt1EJ5m3K7FgoC7ogWVwPbyJ9) As we join our neighbors, friends, and loved ones in service, we can begin to undo the damage wrought by the history of our nation that continues to this day and try to heal from the centuries of racial injustice. We’re going to save the world, and we’re going to do it together. In memory of Dr. King,

name: Monika Johnson-Hostler
subject: What Today Means
disclaimer_text: PAID FOR BY MONIKA FOR CONGRESS
body:
Today we remember the lessons and legacy of Dr. Martin Luther King Jr.

| [](https://monika.bsd.net/page/m/4449d991/7dfbb003/42ab213d/5527c158/3166243578/VEsH/?g=DRs0ITjfd5ldy6_maJSnCeA "https://secure.actblue.com/donate/monika-johnson-hostler-for-congress?refcode=rtw_20191114_em_experience&recurring=auto&refcode2=header")
---
"Darkness cannot drive out darknes, only light can do that. Hate cannot drive out hate, only love can do that." Dr. King dedicated his life working against hate and advocating for justice. Today we reflect on his words and remember his legacy as we continue to fight for equity and equality across our country. We are still fighting for equal access to the ballot box, fighting against income equality, fighting to fulfill the broken promises that we have made to our marginalized communities, and we are still fighting against hate. So, I ask that you join me today and every other day in remembering Dr. King for his message of unity, equality, and justice for all. With Much appreciation, Monika Johnson Hostler Candidate for Congress, North Carolina District 2
---
| | | Monika Johnson-Hostler is running for Congress to give the people of North Carolina's 2nd District a representative who will listen, work hard, and stay true to their word, doing what's best for the district. As the District 2 member of the Wake County school board and Executive Director of North Carolina's Coalition Against Sexual Assault, Monika knows how to lead with solutions. For Monika Johnson-Hostler, serving people is about integrity, and that's what she'll bring to Congress. _****_
Peter,
Before the trial begins, I want to make you aware that the far left is already making up lies and distortions about my position on how the Senate impeachment trial should be conducted. I wanted to send you this personal note so you can hear directly from me how I intend to approach this trial.
I know there are many who are going to try and turn the next few weeks into a circus in Washington, but I will not let that affect how I do my work. I was elected by the people of Maine to do a job -- and I take my responsibilities very seriously.
During the Kavanaugh nomination, I stood up for our Constitutional values and for a fair process. I know that frustrated people at various points, but I believe very deeply that we must protect the integrity of the process. And I intend to take the same approach with this trial. I will advocate for a fair process, I will consider the case put on by both sides, and I will make a decision based on what I think is right. That is what I was elected to do.
In the past, people have tried to bully me into voting one way or another, and I know that over the next few weeks that is likely to happen again. But you have my promise that I will not give in to the pressure, no matter how intense.
I intend to treat the upcoming weeks in a dignified and solemn manner. And then I hope that Congress will return its attention to addressing the nation and its needs and do the work that we were elected to do.
I appreciate the support that you have shown me in the past -- even in the most difficult times -- and I appreciate your taking the time to read this message.
Sincerely,

name: MJ Hegar
subject: Peter, will you join me as a citizen co-sponsor of the constitutional amendment overturning Citizens United?
disclaimer_text: Paid for by MJ for Texas
body:
|
---
[](https://secure.mjfortexas.com/page/m/6489070a/7f51f7bc/4aaa3843/4ef00a1c/3609388349/VEsH/?g=f7VweDpTVzRduSR3BPpGv5A "https://secure.actblue.com/donate/logo?refcode=emaillogo&amount=5")

| | Hey Peter,

If you know the story of how I decided to run for office, you know why campaign finance reform matters so much to me. I went to DC to advocate for opening ground combat roles to women, and it was way harder than it should have been to get my representative to hear me out -- he could make time for lobbyist lunches but not to hear me explain my point of view as a combat veteran.

That is _**not**_ how our democracy should work. And it's a big part of why I decided to run -- so Texans like me would have legislators who actually listen to them, not just their big-money donors. I'm proud to be endorsed by End Citizens United and to work side-by-side with them to end the corrupting influence of dark money on our democracy. [_**Will you join us on this mission by signing on as a citizen co-sponsor of the constitutional amendment overturning _Citizens United_?**_](https://secure.mjfortexas.com/page/m/6489070a/7f51f7bc/4aaa3843/4ef00a13/3609388349/VEsE/?g=f7VweDpTVzRduSR3BPpGv5A "https://sign.actiontaker.org/page/s/CU-anniversary-2020?source=Hegar&redirect_url=https://sign.actiontaker.org/ECU-10-Hegar-AB") **[**SIGN NOW**](https://secure.mjfortexas.com/page/m/6489070a/7f51f7bc/4aaa3843/4ef00a13/3609388349/VEsF/?g=f7VweDpTVzRduSR3BPpGv5A "https://sign.actiontaker.org/page/s/CU-anniversary-2020?source=Hegar&redirect_url=https://sign.actiontaker.org/ECU-10-Hegar-AB")** I'm not taking a single dime from corporate PACs, because I will be accountable to you, not the special interests. Texas knows what it's like to have a senator beholden to corporate interests -- [**and we 've had enough.**](https://secure.mjfortexas.com/page/m/6489070a/7f51f7bc/4aaa3843/4ef00a13/3609388349/VEsC/?g=f7VweDpTVzRduSR3BPpGv5A "https://sign.actiontaker.org/page/s/CU-anniversary-2020?source=Hegar&redirect_url=https://sign.actiontaker.org/ECU-10-Hegar-AB") Sen. Cornyn's special interest pals are already pouring money into Texas, and they'll do whatever it takes to try to reelect their yes-man.
* The dark money-fueled Koch network just announced Texas is one of their top 5 Senate targets in its biggest election-year push ever. Their DC super PAC already has already spent $66,000 to prop up Sen. Cornyn!
* And a shady pro-Cornyn dark money group has spent over $2 million on TV ads in Texas, including ads thanking Sen. Cornyn for standing with them against legislation to lower health care costs.
* Senator Cornyn's super PAC raised more than $1 million since July, with almost all of the money coming from one mega-donor.

Senator Cornyn has taken millions from corporate PACs and special interests, from Big Pharma to the gun lobby, and then followed their lead to kill bills that could make prescription drugs more affordable and protect our children from gun violence. That's because he cares more about helping his donors than fighting for Texans like you and me. Enough is enough. [_**Sign on to be a citizen co-sponsor of the constitutional amendment overturning _Citizens United_ today.**_](https://secure.mjfortexas.com/page/m/6489070a/7f51f7bc/4aaa3843/4ef00a13/3609388349/VEsD/?g=f7VweDpTVzRduSR3BPpGv5A "https://sign.actiontaker.org/page/s/CU-anniversary-2020?source=Hegar&redirect_url=https://sign.actiontaker.org/ECU-10-Hegar-AB")
\-- MJ
**[**DONATE**](https://secure.mjfortexas.com/page/m/6489070a/7f51f7bc/4aaa3843/4ef00a12/3609388349/VEsA/p/eyJKU1ZOUVVsTVNVNUhTVVFsSlE9PSI6Ijc4NyJ9/?g=f7VweDpTVzRduSR3BPpGv5A "https://secure.actblue.com/donate/ecu-10years?refcode=email_20200120.3__787__&amount=10&recurring=true")**

name: Cory Booker
subject: "Life's most persistent and urgent question is: what are you doing for others?"
disclaimer_text: Paid for by Cory Booker for Senate
body:
| | Today is Martin Luther King Jr. Day, a time we set aside to remember an American hero who committed his life to the struggle for civil rights and equality under the law for Black Americans. I believe we can continue to keep Dr. King's memory alive by striving for the ideals that he championed for his entire life. Many of the important lessons he taught us still resonate deeply today -- and many of the causes he fought for are battles we're still fighting right now, more than 50 years after his passing. _We 're still fighting for a criminal justice system that treats everyone equally regardless of their skin color or wealth._ _We 're still fighting to expand voting rights and ensure every American has equal access to the polls._ _We 're still fighting for higher wages and a health care system that provides affordable coverage to everyone._ _We 're still fighting for quality public schools and affordable housing in every neighborhood._ _And we 're still fighting for safe communities that are spared from the tragedy of gun violence._ **These are all battles that we must not stop fighting.** Throughout his life, Dr. King often spoke about the "beloved community." At a point in our history where we're so deeply divided, we must bring forth a spirit of radical love -- a defiant love -- that channels our better angels and overcomes despair with hope. If we can do this, we'll move closer and closer to realizing the inclusive and just society that Dr. King so boldly envisioned. **If we unite by turning our common pain into a common purpose, there are no limits to what we can accomplish together.** I challenge you to find some time today to perform an act of service in your community -- however big or small it may seem. Then, continue the work. Be an agent of change that helps bring us closer to justice and equality. Let us aspire to live up to the extraordinary legacy of Dr. King today and every day thereafter. Together, we will make the dream real. Sincerely,
Cory **_"Darkness cannot drive out darkness; only light can do that. Hate cannot drive out hate; only love can do that. "_**

---

name: =?utf-8?Q?Mike=20Bloomberg?=
subject: A vision for economic justice
disclaimer_text: Paid for by Mike Bloomberg 2020
body:
I'm not running for president to do small things. | |
---
| | | [ ](https://mikebloomberg.us11.list-manage.com/track/click?u=bd4eb7384f3c2a91e96488936&id=d65f5ff03f&e=f54746ec54)
---
| | | Today, as we celebrate the life and legacy of Dr. King, we remember that he not only marched for equal rights, he marched for economic justice -- because true equality is only achieved when there is no correlation between race, ethnicity, and success. Fulfilling Dr. King's vision of economic equality for all people is a monumental challenge -- and I intend to make solving it a centerpiece of my presidency. It won't be easy, but I'm not running for president to do small things. I'm running to do big things that will make a big difference. **I am determined to break the link between the challenge of African-American wealth creation today and the racial inequalities of the past.** For hundreds of years, America systematically stole black lives, black freedom, and black labor. It was a theft of labor and a transfer of wealth -- enshrined in law and enforced by violence. I went to Tulsa yesterday because it represents a stark example of this system. A century ago, the Greenwood neighborhood of Tulsa was a thriving, upper-middle class black community -- until a white mob burned 1,200 homes, looted dozens of shops and left nothing but ruins and rubble across 35 blocks... and massacred more than 200 hundred black residents. Not one white person ever went to jail. It was one of the deadliest and ugliest attacks in American history -- but like many Americans, I had never heard of it. This is a story that never makes it to our history books, and it is just one example. From 1917 to 1923, more than 1,000 black Americans were killed by white mobs in cities and towns all across the country. But the numbers don't even begin to do justice to the story. The impact of this theft over a period of centuries has meant an enormous loss of wealth for individuals and families, across generations. Today, the typical black family in America owns one-tenth of the wealth of the typical white family. One-tenth. **_It's past time to say enough -- and to damn well do something about it!_** Though my father never earned more than $6,000 a year, I've been very lucky in life. I often say my story would only have been possible in America -- and that's true. But I also know that my story likely would have turned out very differently if I had been black -- and that more black Americans of my generation would have ended up with far more wealth, had they been white. Instead, they have had to struggle to overcome great odds, because their families started out further behind, and were excluded from opportunities -- in housing, employment, education, and other areas. So yesterday, I proposed a sweeping and ambitious strategy to invest in black wealth creation and close the racial wealth gap that plagues our country. We're calling it "The Greenwood Initiative," and it has three big goals: **One: We will help one million more black families buy a home,** to counteract the effects of redlining and the subprime mortgage crisis, which are still being felt among many black families. **Two: We will double the number of black-owned businesses,** which are far too few. We'll start by increasing capital for entrepreneurs in black communities and by strengthening enforcement of the Community Reinvestment Act, to make sure that lenders are investing in areas that need it most. **And three: We will help black families triple their wealth over the next 10 years** \-- to an all-time high. Our place-based strategy will invest $70 billion in 100 communities around the country. We will help make sure the quality of a child's education is not tied to their zip code. Just think about this: If we could eliminate the racial wealth gap in this generation, we would add $1.5 trillion to the American economy. Everyone would benefit. So what are we waiting for? **[Show your support or learn more about my comprehensive and inclusive plan for economic equality](https://mikebloomberg.us11.list-manage.com/track/click?u=bd4eb7384f3c2a91e96488936&id=f049207219&e=f54746ec54).**
